1 
PURPOSE AND SCOPE  
1.1 SCOPE OF DOCUMENT 
 
This Installation, Operation, and Maintenance (IOM) document defines the technical description, installation 
requirements, system architecture, operation, and maintenance procedures for the vessel’s Radar System comprising 
the SIMRAD HALO24 Radar Scanner and R3016 16-inch Display Unit. 
The document covers the complete radar installation including the radar antenna/scanner, display unit, 
interconnecting network and power cabling, mounting arrangements, and associated accessories required to provide 
short and medium-range marine radar surveillance for navigation and situational awareness. 
The purpose of this document is to: 
• 
Describe the overall system configuration and equipment layout 
• 
Provide installation and interfacing requirements 
• 
Explain normal operating procedures and user controls 
• 
Define routine inspection, preventive maintenance, and troubleshooting guidelines 
• 
Ensure safe, reliable, and continuous radar operation onboard 
The SIMRAD HALO24 radar system provides real-time target detection, tracking, and navigation support by 
transmitting and receiving radar signals and displaying echoes on the dedicated R3016 16-inch colour monitor. The 
system is intended for use as a navigation aid to enhance vessel safety during routine operations, restricted visibility, 
and coastal or offshore navigation.

1.3 EQUIPMENT DESCRIPTION 
1.3.1 
X-BAND RADAR TRANSCEIVER UNIT SIMRAD- HALO24 
The SIMRAD HALO24 is a high-performance marine radar dome designed to deliver advanced target detection, 
navigation support, and collision awareness from a single compact unit. The HALO24 radar employs pulse compression 
radar technology which combines high resolution with long range and excellent close-in detection performance, 
making it suitable for a wide range of marine navigation scenarios including harbor approaches, coastal waters, 
offshore transits, and low-visibility conditions.

This document is the property of Contractor and should not be distributed, copied, quoted or reproduced in any manner whatsoever, in whole or in part, without the prior 
written consent of Contractor. This document is issued as an “uncontrolled copy” unless over-stamped “CONTROLLED COPY”. 
Operating within the X-band frequency range, the HALO24 has a maximum range of up to 48 nautical miles and can 
detect objects from very short distances to long distance targets, enhancing situational awareness and safe vessel 
operation. The radar rotates at variable speeds up to 60 revolutions per minute (RPM), enabling rapid 360° sweeps 
and near-real-time updates of radar returns. This fast rotation capability is particularly beneficial for close-range 
navigation and collision avoidance. 
 
The HALO24 radar incorporates Velocity Track™ Doppler technology, which uses the Doppler effect to determine the 
relative motion of targets and provides intuitive visual feedback on whether objects are approaching or moving away. 
This feature improves the operator’s ability to identify potential hazards and make informed navigational decisions 
without the need to manually select individual targets. 
 
In addition to Velocity Track™, the HALO24 supports advanced radar modes including simultaneous dual-range 
operation, which allows near and far range scans to be viewed together, and MARPA target tracking (where supported 
by the connected radar display unit), which can automatically track multiple targets and display their speed and course. 
These capabilities further enhance collision avoidance and target monitoring. 
 
The HALO24 radar is designed for durability and reliability in harsh marine environments, with IPX6-rated waterproof 
construction and robust mechanical design. Typical power requirements are suitable for 12 V or 24 V DC vessel 
electrical systems, and standard network interfaces such as Ethernet allow connection to compatible multifunction 
displays (MFDs) or external processing units for radar control and display integration. 
 
Overall, the SIMRAD HALO24 radar combines advanced scanning performance, rapid update rates, and intuitive 
motion sensing technology to provide a versatile and effective radar solution for modern marine navigation and 
situational awareness. 
 
SIMRAD- HALO24 installed at the following location:       
▪ 
NAVIGATION DECK- STARBOARD SIDE (86-RR-601) 
1.3.2 
RADAR CONTROL UNIT WITH DISPLAY SIMRAD R3016 
 
The SIMRAD R3016 is a dedicated radar control and display unit designed to serve as the primary operator interface 
for compatible SIMRAD radar systems, such as the HALO24 Pulse Compression Radar. It integrates a 16-inch 
widescreen color display with an embedded control processor and user interface to present radar echoes, settings, 
and navigational information in an intuitive and accessible format. The R3016 features a 16-inch LED-backlit TFT LCD 
with a resolution of 1366 × 768, providing a wide and detailed plan-position indicator (PPI) presentation essential for 
marine radar interpretation. The display supports multiple languages and on-screen menus, allowing operators to 
adjust radar settings and view target information without obstructing key visual data. 
Control inputs are provided through a combination of integrated direct-access menu keys and a rotary dial interface 
beside the display. These controls enable rapid selection of radar functions, range scales, filters, and tracking features, 
even under challenging marine conditions or while wearing gloves. 
The R3016 is engineered for robust marine use with IPX6 water-resistance and watertight rear connectors, making it 
suitable for flush-mount installation in a pilothouse console or bracket-mount installation where exposure to spray 
and moisture may occur.

Connectivity options include Ethernet for radar data and networking, four NMEA 0183 ports, and NMEA 2000 interface 
for integration with navigation sensors such as heading, speed, and AIS. HDMI output is provided to support an optional 
secondary display or to create a remotely operated clone station at a second helm position. The R3016 operates from 
a standard 12–24 V DC power supply and typically consumes approximately 20 W. The unit is built to operate reliably 
in marine environments from -15 °C to +55 °C and meets international performance and EMC standards applicable to 
small craft radar systems. 
 
SIMRAD- R3016 installed at the following location:       
▪ 
NAVIGATION DECK- WHEELHOUSE (86-RR-603) 
1.3.3 
DIN RAIL POWER SUPPLY UNIT PHOENIX CONTACT QUINT-PS/1AC/24DC/5 
 
The Phoenix Contact QUINT-PS/1AC/24DC/5 is a high-performance DIN-rail mountable industrial 24 V DC power supply 
designed to deliver a stable and reliable DC output for demanding control, automation, and communication systems. 
This power supply belongs to the QUINT POWER series, which is engineered for robust operation in industrial, marine, 
and offshore environments where continuous and secure power delivery is essential. 
The unit accepts a standard single-phase AC input (typically 110–240 V AC) and converts it to a regulated 24 V DC 
output at a rated current of 5 A, providing up to 120 W of DC power to downstream equipment. The QUINT-PS series 
incorporates advanced surge suppression, inrush current limiting, and high efficiency design, ensuring minimal 
interruption even under unstable mains conditions. 
Designed to support critical systems such as communication equipment, PLCs, monitoring devices, sensors, and control 
panels, the QUINT-PS/1AC/24DC/5 offers excellent load regulation, low output ripple, and high immunity to electrical 
noise. It features built-in diagnostic indicators for power and fault status, facilitating rapid visual checks of operational 
health. 
Safety and reliability are enhanced through multiple protective functions, including: 
• 
Overload protection 
• 
Short-circuit protection 
• 
Overvoltage and overtemperature shutdown 
The power supply can withstand harsh industrial environments, with robust construction and thermal management 
that allows continuous operation at full load across a wide ambient temperature range. Its compact design and 
standardized DIN-rail mounting system enable straightforward installation in instrument racks, control cabinets, or 
power distribution panels. 
The QUINT-PS/1AC/24DC/5 power supply is widely used to provide dependable 24 V DC power to electronic systems 
where uninterrupted operation is critical for safety, control, and communications. 
 
Din Rail Power supply unit installed at the following location:   
▪ 
NAVIGATION DECK- WHEELHOUSE 
▪ 
A-DECK – MCR- GMDSS Cabinet

1.3.4 
ETHERNET SWITCH PHOENIX CONTACT 1085173 
The Phoenix Contact Ethernet Switch, Part No. 1085173, is an industrial-grade unmanaged Ethernet switch designed 
for reliable data networking in automation, control, and communication applications. Engineered to support robust 
and uninterrupted data transmission in demanding environments such as marine, offshore, and industrial installations, 
this device enables multiple Ethernet devices to interconnect over a local area network (LAN) with low latency and 
high availability. 
This Ethernet switch provides a set of 10/100 Mbps Fast Ethernet ports for wired network connections, allowing 
sensors, controllers, human machine interfaces (HMIs), communication modules, and monitoring devices to share data 
in real time. As an unmanaged switch, it requires no configuration and supports plug-and-play operation, simplifying 
system integration and reducing commissioning time. 
The unit incorporates industrial hardened design features such as wide operating temperature tolerance, surge 
protection, and vibration-resistant construction to withstand shipboard and factory floor conditions. The robust 
housing and DIN-rail mountable form factor allow secure installation within equipment cabinets or control panels. 
Status LEDs on each port provide visual indication of link presence, activity, and connection speed, supporting quick 
fault detection and maintenance. 
The switch provides the reliable data infrastructure necessary for interconnecting communication equipment, 
automation devices, and monitoring systems within the vessel’s network architecture. It’s simple, maintenance-free 
operation and industrial durability make it ideal for supporting mission-critical network communication where 
consistent performance is essential. 
Ethernet switch installed at the following location:   
▪ 
NAVIGATION DECK- STARBOARD SIDE -JB (86-RR-604)

ETHERNET SWITCH PHOENIX CONTACT 1085173 
The Phoenix Contact Ethernet Switch, Part No. 1085173, is an industrial-grade unmanaged Ethernet switch designed 
for reliable data networking in automation, control, and communication applications. Engineered to support robust 
and uninterrupted data transmission in demanding environments such as marine, offshore, and industrial installations, 
this device enables multiple Ethernet devices to interconnect over a local area network (LAN) with low latency and 
high availability. 
This Ethernet switch provides a set of 10/100 Mbps Fast Ethernet ports for wired network connections, allowing 
sensors, controllers, human machine interfaces (HMIs), communication modules, and monitoring devices to share data 
in real time. As an unmanaged switch, it requires no configuration and supports plug-and-play operation, simplifying 
system integration and reducing commissioning time. 
The unit incorporates industrial hardened design features such as wide operating temperature tolerance, surge 
protection, and vibration-resistant construction to withstand shipboard and factory floor conditions. The robust 
housing and DIN-rail mountable form factor allow secure installation within equipment cabinets or control panels. 
Status LEDs on each port provide visual indication of link presence, activity, and connection speed, supporting quick 
fault detection and maintenance. 
The switch provides the reliable data infrastructure necessary for interconnecting communication equipment, 
automation devices, and monitoring systems within the vessel’s network architecture. It’s simple, maintenance-free 
operation and industrial durability make it ideal for supporting mission-critical network communication where 
consistent performance is essential. 
Ethernet switch installed at the following location:

NAVIGATION DECK- STARBOARD SIDE -JB (86-RR-604)

CHOOSE THE SCANNER LOCATION 
 
The performance of the SIMRAD HALO24 radar is significantly influenced by the mounting location of the scanner. For 
optimum detection and coverage, the radar scanner should be installed at the highest practical position on the vessel, 
clear of obstructions such as masts, funnels, antennas, or deck structures that may block or reflect the radar beam. 
Installing the HALO24 at an elevated position improves the radar horizon and increases the maximum detection range, 
enabling earlier identification of vessels, coastlines, and navigational hazards. However, excessive mounting height 
may create a larger blind area directly around the vessel, where very close targets may not be detected. Additionally, 
higher installation heights can slightly reduce the radar’s ability to detect small targets within heavy sea clutter. 
Therefore, the scanner should be mounted at a balanced height that provides maximum coverage while minimizing 
blind spots and interference, ensuring reliable target detection for safe navigation under all operating conditions. 
 
Do not install the scanner: 
 
▪ 
Too high up which may cause degradation of the radar picture over short ranges. 
▪ 
Close to lamps or exhaust outlets. The heat emissions may damage the dome. Soot and smoke 
will degrade the performance of the radar. 
▪ 
Close to the antennas of other equipment such as direction finders, VHF antennas, GPS 
equipment, as it may cause or be subject to interference. 
▪ 
Where a large obstruction is at the same level as the beam, the obstruction is likely to generate 
false echoes and/or shadow zones. 
▪ 
Where it will be subjected to strong vibrations. Vibrations could degrade the performance or 
service life of the radar. 
▪ 
Directly on to a large flat roof area. Use a pedestal to elevate the scanner for radar beams to 
clear roof line.

For best performance, the radar should be positioned to allow the beams to clear the superstructure of the boat. For 
every increase of 200 mm (7.87”) of dimension (A), increase the height (B) by 80 mm (1.6”). 
 
 
 
• 
 
RADAR SCANNER MOUNTING PROCEDURE 
 
The SIMRAD HALO24 radar scanner is installed on a dedicated steel mast structure at an elevated position above the 
deck to provide unobstructed 360° radar coverage and maximum detection range. The mast arrangement ensures the 
scanner is clear of surrounding structures such as handrails, deck equipment, cranes, and accommodation blocks that 
could otherwise cause shadow sectors or signal reflections. 
Prior to installation, the supplied mounting template shall be positioned and secured on the mast top plate, ensuring 
the scanner front is aligned toward the vessel’s bow. Adequate clearance shall be maintained around the scanner to 
prevent obstruction of the radar beam and to allow safe servicing access. 
 
Use the supplied mounting template and secure it firmly at the selected installation location. Prior to drilling, ensure 
the following: 
• 
The template is correctly oriented so that the front of the radar scanner faces the vessel’s bow. 
• 
A minimum clearance of 315 mm (12.5 in) is maintained in all directions from the scanner center to avoid 
obstruction of the radar beam. 
• 
The mounting surface thickness does not exceed 18 mm (0.7 in). If thicker, longer mounting bolts must be 
used. 
Note: Supplied mounting bolts are M8 × 30 mm. If longer bolts are required, use marine-grade stainless steel hardware 
and ensure a thread engagement between 8 mm and 18 mm.

▪ 
Drill four mounting holes using a 9.5 mm (3/8 in) drill bit according to the template. 
▪ 
Connect the radar scanner interconnection cable before securing the unit. 
▪ 
Route the cable through the designated cable retention channel to prevent strain or damage. 
▪ 
Carefully position the scanner over the drilled holes and align with the threaded mounting points. 
▪ 
Fit a plain washer and lock washer onto each mounting bolt. 
▪ 
Insert and tighten the bolts securely into the scanner mounting holes.(Tightening torque: 12–18 Nm 
(8.9–13.3 lb-ft)) 
▪ 
Install triangular support braces on the mast structure to provide adequate rigidity and minimize 
vibration during vessel operation. 
▪ 
Route all scanner cables vertically along the mast and secure them at regular intervals using clamps or 
saddles to prevent mechanical stress. 
▪ 
Install a junction box below the scanner to provide protected cable termination and allow easy 
maintenance access. 
▪ 
Position the installation to minimize shadowing or obstruction from ship structures and maintain safe 
working clearance for inspection.

Wiring Guidelines 
Do not 
Make sharp bends in the cables 
Run cables in a way that allows water to flow down into the connectors 
Run the data cables adjacent to radar, transmitter, or large/high current carrying cables or high frequency signal

Run cables so they interfere with mechanical systems

connect a mouse line to the outer jacket of the cable so that the strain of pulling is transferred to the stronger outer 
jacket of the cable. Use some small cable ties to secure the mouse line to the outer jacket as well if there is sufficient 
clearance

INSTALLATION INSTRUCTIONS OF RADAR CONTROL UNIT WITH DISPLAY SIMRAD -R3016 
 
The SIMRAD R3016 is a dedicated radar control unit and display designed to serve as the primary operator interface 
for compatible marine radar systems, such as the HALO series. It features an integrated high-definition 16-inch 
widescreen display that presents a clear and comprehensive radar picture for navigation and situational awareness.The 
unit incorporates an intuitive keypad and rotary dial interface with direct-access menu keys to provide reliable control 
of radar functions even in challenging sea conditions. It supports versatile installation options, including flush or 
bracket mounting, and is engineered for ease of installation and day-to-day operation on board.

3.2 OVERALL SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The radar system is arranged as an integrated network comprising a mast-mounted scanner, deck-level junction and 
termination facilities, centralized power distribution, and an indoor control and display position located within the 
navigation spaces. The architecture is designed to provide reliable radar operation, simplified cable routing, and 
flexibility for both commissioning and final operational configuration. 
The radar scanner is installed externally at the navigation deck starboard side mast head to achieve maximum radar 
horizon and unobstructed 360° coverage. From the scanner, a combined power and Ethernet communication cable is 
routed vertically along the mast and secured at regular intervals to prevent mechanical stress and vibration. The cable 
is led into a nearby weather-protected junction box where termination and distribution of power and data connections 
are performed. 
From the junction box, the Ethernet communication line and DC power supply are extended through marine-grade 
cables to the indoor equipment locations. The Ethernet link carries both radar video data and control signals between

the scanner and the radar control/display unit, while the dedicated DC supply provides stable operating power to the 
radar equipment. 
Primary AC power is sourced from the vessel’s 230 VAC UPS distribution board. This supply is routed through protective 
breakers and converted to the required DC voltage before being delivered to the radar system, ensuring continuous 
and protected operation during both normal and backup power conditions. 
The radar control and display position is presently installed at the Navigation Deck Wheelhouse to support 
commissioning, testing, and navigation during construction and transit. A permanent provision for the control unit is 
included within the GMDSS console. Upon FPSO anchoring and commencement of operational service, the control unit 
will be relocated from the wheelhouse to the GMDSS panel, utilizing the pre-installed cabling and interfaces without 
modification to the external scanner or junction arrangements. 
Navigation sensor inputs, including heading and other interfaced signals, are routed to the control position to enable 
stabilized and accurate radar presentation. All cabling is arranged through dedicated trays and conduits to maintain 
segregation between power and signal circuits, reduce interference, and allow safe maintenance access. 
This distributed architecture ensures: 
• 
Clear radar coverage from an elevated scanner position 
• 
Short and protected external cable runs 
• 
Centralized indoor control and monitoring 
• 
Reliable power from the UPS supply 
• 
Flexible relocation of the display/control unit 
• 
Simplified maintenance through junction and termination points 
The overall design therefore provides a robust, serviceable, and operationally adaptable radar installation suitable for 
both temporary navigation use and permanent FPSO operational deployment

Stabilization Modes 
 
Stabilization mode compensates for vessel movement to maintain a steady and accurate radar picture. The system 
uses speed information from connected sensors to stabilize the radar display either relative to the sea surface or the 
ground. 
 
Sea Stabilization 
• 
Uses speed through water from the Speed Log. 
• 
Radar picture is referenced to the water surface. 
• 
Suitable for manoeuvring and short-range navigation. 
• 
May be affected by tide and current, reducing ground position accuracy. 
Ground Stabilization 
• 
Uses speed over ground from EPFS/GPS. 
• 
Radar picture is referenced to the earth/ground. 
• 
Fixed objects remain stationary on the display. 
• 
Provides more accurate target tracking and collision avoidance. 
• 
Recommended for normal navigation and offshore operation. 
Operation 
• 
Select the preferred speed source from the Speed Source menu. 
• 
The radar automatically applies the corresponding stabilization mode. 
• 
Active mode and source are shown on the display. 
 
 
Primary speed source (A), secondary speed source (B) and stabilization mode (C) are shown in the Own ship 
information panel.

Network Settings

Device Name: Give a unique name if multiple units are installed.

3.4 MAINTENANCE 
3.4.1 
MAINTENANCE OF SIMRAD- HALO24 
Clean the radome using soapy water and a soft cloth. Avoid using abrasive cleaning products. Do not use solvents 
such as gasoline, acetone, M.E.K etc. as this will damage the dome surface. 
3.4.2 
MAINTENANCE OF R3016 RADAR CONTROL UNIT 
•
Preventive Maintenance
▪
The unit contains no user-serviceable internal parts. Only basic external inspection and cleaning are
required.
•
General Care
▪
Fit the protective sun cover when the unit is not in use.
▪
Protect the unit from prolonged UV exposure and harsh weather.
•
Display Cleaning
▪
Clean screen with a soft cloth and fresh water to remove salt.
▪
For stains, use 50/50 warm water and isopropyl alcohol.
▪
Do not use solvents, ammonia, or abrasive cleaners.
▪
Apply minimal pressure to avoid scratching.
•
Media Port Door
▪
Clean regularly to prevent salt buildup and water ingress.
•
Keys and Controls
▪
Check that keys move freely and are not stuck.
▪
Free any jammed keys gently.
•
Connectors
▪
Inspect visually for damage or looseness.
▪
Ensure plugs are fully inserted and locked.
•
Data Logging
▪
System automatically records NMEA data logs for troubleshooting.
▪
Logs can be exported to external storage when required.

3.5 TROUBLESHOOTING GUIDE  
 
The radar control unit continuously monitors the system for faults, danger situations, and operational alerts while 
running. When an alert condition occurs, it is displayed in the Alerts panel along with a descriptive text and appropriate 
icon. Alerts remain visible until the underlying condition is resolved and acknowledged by the operator. 
 
There are three main alert types with differing priorities and indications:  
• 
Alarms: Highest priority conditions that may affect safety or system performance. 
▪ 
Accompanied by an audible signal. 
▪ 
The alert icon flashes until acknowledged. 
• 
Warnings: Medium priority conditions indicating issues that may lead to problems if unaddressed. 
▪ 
Accompanied by periodic audible signals. 
▪ 
The warning icon flashes until acknowledged. 
• 
Cautions: Informational notifications that require attention but do not trigger audible alerts. 
▪ 
Display only a steady icon and descriptive text 
 
When an alert is triggered, the alert appears in the Alerts panel. Alarms and warnings remain in the Alerts panel until 
the reason for the alarm/warning is removed and the alarm/warning has been acknowledged.

The radar control unit continuously monitors the system for faults, danger situations, and operational alerts while 
running. When an alert condition occurs, it is displayed in the Alerts panel along with a descriptive text and appropriate 
icon. Alerts remain visible until the underlying condition is resolved and acknowledged by the operator. 
 
There are three main alert types with differing priorities and indications:  
• 
Alarms: Highest priority conditions that may affect safety or system performance. 
▪ 
Accompanied by an audible signal. 
▪ 
The alert icon flashes until acknowledged. 
• 
Warnings: Medium priority conditions indicating issues that may lead to problems if unaddressed. 
▪ 
Accompanied by periodic audible signals. 
▪ 
The warning icon flashes until acknowledged. 
• 
Cautions: Informational notifications that require attention but do not trigger audible alerts. 
▪ 
Display only a steady icon and descriptive text 
 
When an alert is triggered, the alert appears in the Alerts panel. Alarms and warnings remain in the Alerts panel until 
the reason for the alarm/warning is removed and the alarm/warning has been acknowledged.
